critic_raw:
CRITIQUE:

1. LOW-VOLUME STORE PERFORMANCE GAP: 19.4% MAPE for bottom-quintile stores is more than twice the acceptable range seen in higher-volume stores. For perishable goods, this level of forecasting error creates operational problems regardless of the low aggregate sales share. Full autonomy for these stores is not supported by the performance data.

2. NO HUMAN OVERRIDE — PERISHABLE RISK: Eliminating override capability entirely for perishable goods is architecturally risky. Demand spikes from local events, weather disruptions, or supply anomalies require the ability to override automated orders. Without override, there is no recovery mechanism for time-sensitive perishable order errors.

Both concerns are valid and actionable. They argue for tiered deployment and exception handling, not model rejection.

Verdict: MIXED.
defender_raw:
DEFENSE:

1. Proportionality of low-volume impact: under 3% of sales. MAPE degradation is real but aggregate impact is bounded. Rule-based system may perform no better.

2. Override gap: Concede. Some form of exception handling is necessary for perishable goods management. Automated order thresholds or seasonal override flags would address this.

The model is appropriate for high-volume stores under full autonomy. Low-volume stores and exception handling require design modifications.
adjudication_raw: Both concerns confirmed as genuine. Low-volume MAPE gap argues for tiered deployment. No-override design needs exception handling mechanism for perishables. Mixed verdict — the model is deployable with modifications, not as currently proposed.
